Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ha, Meem. The revelation of the Book is from Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise. (1-2)

The Qur'anic name of this Surah is also 'Ha-Meem'. We have pointed out in the commentary of the preceding Surahs that the sharing of names among the Surahs is proof of the sharing of their contents. Thus, all 'Ha-Meem' Surahs which you have been reading share the same common denominator. If there is any difference, it is in the style of expression, method of reasoning, and brevity or detail.

The Preface of the Surah

'The revelation of the Book...'—like the preceding Surah, the preface of this Surah also refers to the greatness and importance of the Qur'an; however, the aspect of describing its importance is different from the preceding Surah. We have explained the analysis of the word 'Tanzeel' in its proper place—stating that its meaning is not merely sending down, but sending down with elaborate care, and the special care which Allah showed to send down the Qur'an has been explained in various places. Along with mentioning this special care, Allah Almighty has referred to two of His attributes: 'Al-Aziz' (Exalted in Might) and 'Al-Hakeem' (Wise). 'Al-Aziz' means dominant and powerful, and 'Al-Hakeem' means the One in Whose every word and action lies wisdom. Combining these two attributes clarifies two matters:

The Requirements of the Attributes of being Exalted in Might and Wise

First, the God Who has sent down this Book with this care is no weak, frail, and helpless being, but the sovereignty and power of the entire universe are in His hand alone. This is not the request of a petitioner, but the command of the true Sovereign of the entire universe which must be obeyed; if its due respect is not shown, let people remember that when He seizes them, no one can save them.

Second, along with being Exalted in Might and Dominant, He is also Wise. For this reason, His every word and action is based on wisdom. The glory of His wisdom is obvious from His wise Book, provided people reflect on it. It is of the glory of His wisdom that although the ungrateful show disrespect and deny His Book and His messenger, He, despite being Dominant and Powerful, does not hasten in seizing them, but continues to grant them respite so that those who possess capability may reform themselves to become deserving of His mercy, and those who have destroyed their capabilities have the proof of Allah fully established against them, leaving them with no excuse on the Day of Judgment.

"Indeed, within the heavens and the earth are signs for the believers." (3)

The Invitation of the Qur'an

This points to the signs of the horizons in support of the Qur'an's invitation to Tawhid and the Resurrection, so that its being a wise discourse is made clear. It states: that to which the Qur'an invites and of which it warns has its signs present in every corner of the heavens and the earth, but these signs are of use only to those who are ready to believe. Those who do not want to believe remain blind even after seeing everything, demanding new signs, and if any new sign is shown to them, they are not convinced by it, but demand another. The primary thing is human will. If the quest for truth and the desire for the destination is present within him, the Creator of the universe has left no deficiency in pointing out the destination. In the heavens, signs are manifest, and the earth also shows the path at every step; but those who want to wander in following their desires see neither the signs of the heavens nor of the earth, and spend their entire lives wandering.

"And in the creation of yourselves and what He dispersed of moving creatures are signs for a people who are certain [in faith]." (4)

Pointing to the Signs present within Human Creation

After making a comprehensive reference to the signs of the heavens and the earth, He drew attention to human creation and the arrangement Allah Almighty has made for his sustenance in this earth—stating that if man reflects on all the stages of his creation, such realities of the Creator's power, wisdom, and lordship will become clear to him that no doubt will remain regarding His Tawhid, the possibility of the Resurrection, and the necessity of reward and punishment. The Creator Who created man from dust and then gave him such an excellent form from a drop of water, why would He be unable to recreate him? The Sustainer Who has arranged for his sustenance from the very initial stage of his creation until the final stage, how can He leave him entirely without responsibility? Why would He not question him regarding those high capabilities He has blessed him with? And why would he associate any other partner with God, when He has made all opposites and components of this universe compatible with him?

Pointing to the Signs of Sustenance

'and what He dispersed of moving creatures' points to that arrangement of lordship which Allah Almighty has made for man in the form of cattle. If man reflects, there is no doubt that the various benefits he obtains from cattle are obtained not by accident, but Allah Almighty brought them into existence specifically for these benefits and made them suitable for them. He made man in need of riding and carrying burdens, so He created suitable beasts for this; He made him in need of milk, meat, hide, and wool, so He gave suitable cattle for these. This is clear proof that the Creator of this universe is highly Merciful, and His gratitude is obligatory. Then, from this, it also follows: since He has blessed man with His favors with this care, it is necessary that a Day comes in which He questions people regarding these blessings—rewarding those who recognized their right, and punishing those who forgot God upon receiving them.

To Believe, the Primary Thing Is the Desire to Believe

'for a people who are certain' (li-qawmin yooqinoon)—'yooqinoon' is used in the sense of intending to believe. That is: although proofs of Tawhid, the Resurrection, and reward and punishment are countless within these things, the mere existence of proofs is of no benefit to the one who does not intend to accept them. The one who does not want to believe in a reality can fabricate doubts even against the most obvious truth.

"And [in] the alternation of night and day and [in] what Allah sends down from the sky of provision [i.e., rain] and gives life thereby to the earth after its lifelessness and [in His] directing of the winds are signs for a people who reason." (5)

'Alternation' (ikhtilaf) here means the coming of night and day one after another. At the same time, this word is also a subtle allusion to the difference in their characteristics, which is necessary for the development, benefit, and survival of this universe. By 'provision' (rizq), rain is meant, which is the source of provision. It is as if the effect is used to refer to the cause, which is well-known in Arabic and other languages.

The Signs of the Alternation of Night and Day

The proof of the alternation of night and day has occurred in various places—showing that although they apparently bear the relation of opposites, yet a compatibility of pairs is found between them for the sustenance of man. This is clear proof that both are created by the same God, and by His command, with full discipline of time, are active in performing their duties. If they had been the creations of different gods, this compatibility could not exist, and if this was absent, life would be impossible for the inhabitants of the earth.

The Signs of Rain

The same is the case with rain—it is sent down from the sky, but gives life to the earth and the people of the earth. This is clear proof that a single will is operating in both the heavens and the earth. If the god of the heavens was different and of the earth different, why would the heaven send down the provision for the people of the earth! Moreover, this is observed by everyone in every season—showing that the earth lies dry, with no sign of life anywhere, until a drop of rain falls and every corner of the earth flourishes immediately. So, the God Whose glory we observe in every season, if He wishes to raise this world alive after its death, why would He face any difficulty in this?

The Signs of the Directing of the Winds

'and [in His] directing of the winds'—meaning the signs of God's power, mercy, lordship, and His punishment are present in the directing of the winds. It clearly appears that their reins are in the hand of a single Being, Who uses them in accordance with His wisdom. If He should withhold them, the entire world would be destroyed in the blink of an eye. He can make them a mercy for one nation, and a punishment for another; by this wind, Allah Almighty saved Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and his people, and destroyed Pharaoh and his people. It is observed daily: farmers are completely satisfied with their crops, but suddenly some wind blows to turn their future dark. Sailors and farmers wait for a favorable wind, but it is in the control of no one besides Allah. In this era, although man has assumed that he has controlled clouds and wind through science, when nature shakes him even slightly, his claim is exposed. These are clear testimonies that only a single Being is ruling over all elements of this universe. Without His permission, not even a leaf can move.

'are signs for a people who reason'—meaning signs of Tawhid and the Resurrection are present at every step, but they are observed by those who utilize their intellect. Those who do not utilize their intellect, or use it only for their material needs, remain deprived of observing the true beauty of these signs.

Since the Qur'an has made brief references to these signs, we also limit our discussion to brief references. These matters have passed in detail in the previous Surahs, and we have explained them.

"These are the signs of Allah which We recite to you in truth; then in what statement after Allah and His signs will they believe?" (6)

Consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) and Reproach to the Opponents

This consoled the Prophet (pbuh) and reproached his opponents. 'These' (Tilka) points to those signs of the horizons and ourselves which were mentioned in the preceding verses. It states: these are the signs of Allah's Tawhid, His power, His wisdom, and His Day of recompense which We are reciting to you through this Qur'an with their correct results and requirements. These signs are so clear that no wise person can deny them. The Qur'an invites to accept their actual requirements. If your opponents are not ready to accept the obvious results of these signs, then what other statement is there which is more rational and satisfying for intellect on which they will believe!

The Meaning of 'in truth' (bil-haqq)

In 'We recite to you in truth' (natlooha 'alayka bil-haqq), 'truth' refers to those certain and actual results which emerge from reflecting on these signs. This points to the reality: although others also reflect on these signs, they do so from their own specific and highly limited perspective; because of this, either they do not reach those realities hidden within them, or they do reach them but since they are contrary to their desires, they turn away from their confession. For instance, astronomers and geologists reflect on the signs of the heavens and the earth, anatomists on human creation, zoologists on animals, meteorologists on night, day, rain, and winds, but due to their narrow-mindedness, they see the grain of dust but cannot see the sun. The meteorologists can predict that the weather will remain warm and dry for the next twenty-four hours and give some explanation for it. Often, their prediction is correct and their explanation accepted, but their eye remains limited only to estimating the winds' action and its effects. Beyond this, they do not reflect on who the actual Controller of these activities is, and what His rights and duties are. Whereas, all these activities occur specifically to make man reach this actual question, solve it, and accept the solution if some servant of God shows it. The Qur'an has exposed specifically those aspects of these signs which reveal the actual reality; for this reason, it described it as: 'We recite to you in truth.'

Two Important Realities

First, this clarified that the Qur'an's invitation is based not on force or authority, but entirely on the clear proofs of the horizons, ourselves, reason, and nature. Those who do not accept do not do so because they are hidden, but because they find them contrary to their desires.

Second, this clarified that the most obvious thing in this universe is Allah and His signs. Those who deny them are not capable of accepting any reality. They are merely slaves of their desires, and worshippers of their stomachs. If such people demand some new signs and miracles, their demands do not deserve attention. No miracle can open the eyes of such blind people.

"Woe to every sinful liar Who hears the verses of Allah recited to him, then persists arrogantly as if he had not heard them. So give him glad tidings of a painful punishment." (7-8)

This is a further extension of the reproach—showing that destruction is for those who have distorted all realities to justify their sinful life.

'Affak' (liar) and 'Athim' (sinful)

'Affak' is the one who distorts the reality of things—meaning while the signs and verses of Allah are pointing to some other reality, he, in following the desires of the self, completely distorts that reality. Its primary referents were the polytheists of the Quraysh, who distorted the religion of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) purely in following their desires, but its general referent includes all those in every era who commit distortions in the verses and commands of Allah under their desires.

'Athim' means sinful, particularly the one who violates rights and duties. Conjoining this with 'affak' shows that those who commit this conspiracy of distorting realities are those wicked ones who want to avoid performing His rights and duties and persist on their life of disobedience. It states: destruction is for these unfortunate ones, because the verses of Allah are being recited to them, but they, out of great pride, listen to them and depart as if they heard nothing. This points to that attitude of the leaders of the Quraysh which they adopted in response to the Prophet's (pbuh) invitation. First, they considered going to his gathering a disgrace, but even if they did, or when the Prophet (pbuh) came to them himself to recite the Qur'an, they departed as if they heard nothing.

By 'recited to him' (tutla 'alayhi), the gravity of their crime is expressed—showing that if the invitation did not reach someone, they might be excused to some extent, but what answer will that unfortunate one give before God at whose ears the messenger himself delivered the call, yet he did not wake up!

Arrogance Is the Primary Cause of Persisting on Shirk

In 'then persists arrogantly' (thumma yusirru mustakbiran), light is shed on the primary cause of persisting on shirk—showing that their persistence is not because the reality was not clear to them, but because they considered it a disgrace that, being the chiefs and leaders of the nation, they accepted the superiority of a person who was not their equal in worldly status.

'So give him glad tidings of a painful punishment'—this explains that 'woe' (wayl) mentioned above: for all such arrogant ones is a painful punishment. If they, out of their pride, have closed their ears to the glad tidings of success, then let them be; deliver to them the glad tidings of the punishment, which is destined for them.

"And when he knows anything of Our signs, he takes them in ridicule. Those will have a humiliating punishment." (9)

A Cheap Weapon to Oppose the Truth

In the preceding verse, that attitude of the arrogant was described which they adopted when the Prophet (pbuh) tried to recite the Qur'an to them. Now, that attitude of theirs is described which they adopted when they came to know of some verse through some other medium. It states: when they come to know of any of Our signs, they try to take it in ridicule so that others are not influenced by it. It should be kept in mind that during the period of the Qur'an's sending down, there were those among the Quraysh who listened to the verses with an impartial mind and were influenced by them. Such people tried to bring these verses to their leaders' knowledge to find out their opinion. Their leaders immediately sensed that people were being influenced by them. To protect people from this influence, they could not say that the Qur'an was a foolish and meaningless book. If they had said so, their common people would have declared them foolish—showing that they were trying to throw dust upon the sun; however, the only thing they could do was to make fun of the Qur'an's statement so that the matter flew away in the wind, and no influence remained on anyone. This kind of cheap activity is not highly effective, but temporarily, people of weak opinions fall into misconceptions. This weapon has been used by the devils of every era against the truth.

The Most Severe Punishment Is That with Disgrace

'Those will have a humiliating punishment'—since the primary motivator behind this attitude of theirs was arrogance, as was mentioned in the preceding verse, He stated: for them is a humiliating punishment. They showed pride against the truth; therefore, for them is disgrace in the Hereafter. It should be kept in mind that although every punishment of Allah is a thing to seek refuge from, His most severe punishment is that which is with disgrace. This is specific to the arrogant.

"Before them is Hell, and what they had earned will not avail them at all, nor what they had taken besides Allah as protectors. And they will have a great punishment." (10)

The meaning of 'min wara'i' (before/after) is determined by the context. Here, it means 'before'—stating: before them is Hell, in which they will fall, and after falling, neither will those earnings of theirs be of any use which became the cause of their arrogance and turning away, nor those partners whom they took as protectors besides Allah.

'And they will have a great punishment'—and the punishment will be no ordinary thing, but a highly great punishment. Its terror cannot be estimated today.

"This [Qur'an] is guidance. And those who have disbelieved in the verses of their Lord will have a painful punishment of filth." (11)

That is, this Qur'an is guidance. It is no plaything. Those who deny or mock His signs must remember that for them will be a painful punishment of filth (rajz aleem). 'Filth' (rajz) clarifies the nature of this punishment—meaning that which causes a shudder. The meaning is: this will be no ordinary punishment, but a highly painful one, which will make hearts tremble.

"It is Allah who subjected to you the sea that ships may sail upon it by His command and that you may seek of His bounty; and perhaps you will be grateful. And He has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, all from Him. Indeed in that are signs for a people who give thought." (12-13)

Resuming the Chain of Proofs

The preceding four verses came in the middle as warning and reminding so that the leaders of the Quraysh were warned and the Prophet (pbuh) consoled; in this verse, the actual sequence of the discourse is taken up again. It states: He alone has subjected the sea to you, such that it carries your ships upon its chest. 'by His command' (bi-amrihi) means this occurs specifically by His command. If it were not by His command, you observe that things smaller than a ship sink immediately in the sea, but a ship carrying thousands of tons sails upon it. This is the law of Allah: a small piece of iron sinks, but a ship carrying thousands of tons of iron does not sink.

Before 'and that you may seek' (wa-litabtaghoo), some words are omitted due to contextual clarity—implying: We made this arrangement so that you travel from one place to another to seek His provision through trade.

The Actual Lesson of the Observations of Lordship

'and perhaps you will be grateful'—this is that actual lesson which everyone whose intellect and conscience are alive derives from these observations of His lordship. It states: He has shown you these signs of His lordship so that you become His grateful servants. It should be kept in mind that this feeling of gratitude is the foundation of His worship. This has been explained in Surah Al-Fatihah.

'And He has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, all from Him'—first, He specifically mentioned the subjection of the sea, which is most prominent and apparently completely uncontrollable. Now, after the specific, He referred to the general signs of His creation—showing: He has also subjected other things of the heavens and the earth to provide benefit to you. 'all from Him' (jamee'an minhu)—meaning all these things are performing service to you by His command, directly or indirectly. 'Jamee'an' (all) relates not to what was before, but to 'minhu' (from Him). Neither have you subdued any of these things, nor has anyone else subdued them, nor are they serving you of their own accord; but whatever is happening, is happening by the planning of your Lord, because He alone is the Creator and Sovereign over all these things.

'Indeed in that are signs for a people who give thought'—meaning for those who reflect, there are various proofs of Tawhid and the Resurrection in the signs of the horizons.

The Proofs of Tawhid and the Resurrection in the Signs of the Horizons

* Their being subjected is proof: none of them has the status of a deity to be worshipped, but every single thing testifies by its very existence that its rein is in the hand of a supreme power which uses it under its wisdom.

* Within them, alongside apparent opposition, compatibility is found, like the compatibility of the parts of a machine. This harmony is proof: only a single will is dominant over this entire system.

* Within this system are testimonies of lordship, mercy, and wisdom which are proof: no blind power is running it. His knowledge, His mercy, and His wisdom require that He brings a Day of Recompense to reward those who recognized His blessings and remained grateful, and to punish those who disregarded them and fell into shirk. If He does not do so, it means the ungrateful and the grateful are equal in His sight, which is contrary to His attributes.

There is no shortage of proofs; what is required is reflection.

At the end, 'people who give thought' (yatafakkaroon) clarifies: there is no shortage of proofs, and the Qur'an has explained them in detail; what is lacking is reflection. People do not reflect on Allah's signs, and reflecting is man's own work. Allah Almighty does not force anyone to do so.

"Say, [O Muhammad], to those who have believed to forgive those who do not expect the days of Allah that He may recompense a people for what they used to earn." (14)

'Yaghfiroo' (forgive) is used here in the sense of overlooking and ignoring.

'The days of Allah' (Ayyam-Allah) refers to those historical days of Allah's justice and retribution in which He destroyed the deniers of His messengers. In the history of nations, these days have been mentioned, and further details will follow. In 'that He may recompense a people' (liyajziya qawman), 'people' (qawm) refers to these opponents. The indefiniteness (tankeer) of this word is to express contempt and aversion, like the indefiniteness of 'quloob' (hearts) in 'upon their hearts are locks'. Those who have interpreted 'people' as the Muslims have not reflected on the context and eloquence.

Just as in verse 6, the Prophet (pbuh) was consoled, in this verse, the believers are consoled: 'Do not be demoralized by the opponents' activities, but overlook them.' Since they do not fear that Day of which they are being warned, they are growing bolder, but Allah is granting respite to this contemptible nation so that they fully fill their cup and face the full punishment. The Sunnah of Allah is: He grants full respite to the wicked so that proof is fully established against them.

"Whoever does a good deed - it is for himself; and whoever does evil - it is against it. Then to your Lord you will be returned." (15)

This explains the preceding theme—showing: if they choose the path of goodness, its benefit will be achieved by them alone; and if they persist on evil, its consequence will fall upon them alone. No responsibility will lie upon the believers, when they have delivered the truth. Then, on the Day of Judgment, everyone will present themselves before God, and the decision will be made as to who was upon the truth and who was upon falsehood.

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 16–23

Next, the attitude which the Jews adopted in support of the polytheists in their hostility against Islam is discussed. During this period, they had also openly supported the polytheists. Since they had held the status of a religious group, their support increased the polytheists' confidence. This made it necessary to expose them so that no fear arose among the Muslims, and the polytheists also understood that those upon whose support they relied were greater criminals. If they followed their word, both would face the same end. Within this, the Prophet (pbuh) is instructed to remain firm on his stance of truth: 'You are now upon the actual Shariah of Allah. Neither do you have to care about the Jews nor about the polytheists. Allah's support is only for you and your companions, and Allah's support is sufficient.' Recite the verses.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"And We certainly gave the Children of Israel the Scripture and judgement and prophethood, and We provided them with good things and preferred them over the worlds." (16)

The Favors Allah Bestowed upon the Children of Israel, which They Disregarded

This refers to those great favors Allah Almighty bestowed upon the Children of Israel, and the objective, as we pointed out, is to show: Allah Almighty bestowed great favors upon them, but they proved so ungrateful that they disregarded every favor, and having deprived themselves, want to see others deprived as well.

'The Scripture' (Al-Kitab) refers, obviously, to the Torah.

'Judgement' (Al-Hukm) is, as the context indicates, that authority and sovereignty which the Children of Israel achieved during the times of Hazrat Dawud and Hazrat Sulaiman (peace be upon them), and which remained for a long period. There is a connection of body and soul between the Book and authority—the Book is the collection of His commands and laws; therefore, the nation which receives His Book necessarily receives authority as well, because authority is essential for the execution of His laws. A nation is stripped of this authority only when it throws His commands behind its back.

'Prophethood' (Al-Nubuwwah) is clear, but its mention here has a specific aspect: after Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), Allah Almighty established a continuous line of prophethood among them which remained until Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him). After the treatment they showed to Hazrat Isa, they were cursed and deprived of both authority and prophethood forever.

'and We provided them with good things' points to that abundance of provision and favor which began after their conquest of Palestine, and reached its peak during the time of Hazrat Sulaiman (peace be upon him).

'and preferred them over the worlds'—this preference is the logical consequence of being given the Book of Allah. The Book of Allah is the light of guidance for creation. When Allah Almighty gives this light to a nation, it means He chose them for guiding His creation. This is indeed a great favor, but this is conditional upon their performing their duty of guiding His creation. When they abandoned this duty, they were deprived of this preference.

"And We gave them clear proofs of the matter [i.e., religion]. And they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them - out of jealous animosity between themselves. Indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ." (17)

Further Care for Their Guidance, and Their Disregard of It

'The matter' (Al-Amr) refers to the Shariah and religion. It states: We gave them the commands of the Shariah in highly clear and un-doubtful form so that no room for differences remained. But despite all this care, they created differences, and these differences arose not because there was any cause, but after the light of knowledge had come to them, they differed purely out of mutual envy; consequently, they were deprived of His light. Now, on the Day of Judgment, He will judge their differences—showing to what extent they were justified, and to what extent their stubbornness and desire to defeat the other was involved.

Consolation for the Muslims and a Warning to the Quraysh

By pointing to this aspect of their character, the objective is, on one hand, to console the Muslims—showing: those who have stood up as judges to decide whether the Quraysh are upon the truth or the Muslims, their own state is that they have been deprived of Allah's religion due to their mutual envy. If such unfortunate people, out of envy, stand up in your opposition, it is nothing surprising.

On the other hand, it is to warn the Quraysh: do not expect of those who have blinded their own eyes to bring you any light; they only desire that just as they are wandering in the darkness after blowing out the light of Allah, you also remain deprived of this light to wander in the darkness in which you have been wandering.

This clarified: differences can occur in understanding, but if the motivator behind these differences is mutual rivalry and the desire to defeat one another, this completely destroys the religion. This is that very same nature of differences which deprived the People of the Book of His light, and which destroyed the Muslims.

"Then We put you, [O Muhammad], on an ordained way [i.e., Shariah] of the matter [i.e., religion]; so follow it and do not follow the desires of those who do not know." (18)

Instruction to the Prophet (pbuh) to Remain Firm on the True Religion of Allah

'Shariah' means a clear path, and 'of the matter' (min al-amr) has occurred in the same sense in this verse as in the preceding verse. It states: when these People of the Book lost the straight path, We mبعوث you upon Our clear Shariah. The meaning is: if today the Children of Israel are whispering: 'When the Torah and their Shariah are present, what was the need for a new Book and a new Shariah?', this is because they do not look at their own deeds. If they did, they would understand why you were mبعوث.

'so follow it and do not follow the desires of those who do not know'—'Ahwa'' (desires) refers, as has been explained, to innovations. Since innovations are introduced to give authorization to desires, the Qur'an has referred to them as 'ahwa'' to point to their source: they are born of desires, not of religion or reason.

This instructed the Prophet (pbuh): follow this clear Shariah which Allah has given you, and do not follow the innovations of those who do not know. Those who do not know include the polytheists, the Jews, and the Christians. The Arab polytheists were unlettered, unfamiliar with the Book and Shariah. They claimed to follow the religion of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), but they possessed only those innovations which their ancestors had fabricated. In their defense, they fought with the Prophet (pbuh)—claiming that he was trying to wipe out their ancestral religion. Although the Jews and Christians claimed to hold the Book, as was described in the preceding verse, they had created so many differences in the religion of Allah that the actual reality was lost. When the Qur'an clarified realities, and these were contrary to their innovations, they became enraged—realizing that this invitation threatened their business; whereas the Qur'an was clarifying the actual religion of the prophets. But since they were entirely in the darkness, they stood as the enemies of the Prophet (pbuh), supporting the Quraysh to wipe out Islam. The Qur'an instructed the Prophet (pbuh) in this verse: 'You are upon the Shariah of Allah. No matter how much they try, remain firm upon it, and never follow their innovations.'

This instruction was given not because there was any fear that you would follow their innovations, but to express aversion to their innovations. Allah Almighty did not think it fit to address them; therefore, He addressed His Prophet (pbuh).

"Indeed, they will never avail you against Allah at all. And indeed, the wrongdoers are allies of one another, but Allah is the ally of the righteous." (19)

No matter how much they try, do not care about them. They cannot be of any use before God. You will be questioned regarding your duty, and they will be questioned regarding theirs; therefore, comply with the command of Allah. Do not care about them, whether they oppose you to suppress you, or try to soften you. Keep in mind: when the believers have Allah as their Protector, they need not fear anyone. When Allah is the helper, who can defeat them!

"This [Qur'an] is enlightenment for mankind and a guidance and mercy for a people who are certain [in faith]." (20)

The Verses of the Qur'an are Enlightening, Provided People Open Their Eyes

This points to the Qur'an, against which the People of the Book and the polytheists formed that coalition. In verse 11 above, its mention has passed. 'This' (Hadha) points to the Qur'an as a whole, and 'enlightenment' (basa'ir) is used in view of the verses of the Qur'an, which the disbelievers mocked. It states: this Qur'an is a collection of enlightening verses. If people mock and oppose it, they only throw dust in their own eyes. This style clarifies: the enlightening nature of the Qur'an is as obvious as the sun. Allah Almighty has sent it down with this purpose, but only those will benefit from it who open their eyes.

'and a guidance and mercy for a people who are certain'—after the general, this is the specific benefit: for those who believe in its warnings and glad tidings, it is guidance and mercy. We have explained: 'guidance' and 'mercy' are used in different meanings in this context—meaning: for those who believe, the Qur'an is guidance in the world, and mercy in the Hereafter. It guides to the straight path in the world, and opens His mercies in the Hereafter.

"Or do those who commit evil deeds think that We will make them like those who have believed and done righteous deeds - [make them] equal in their life and their death? Evil is that which they judge." (21)

The Deniers' Concept of God Is Baseless

That is: those who mock the warnings and glad tidings of the Qur'an assume that in the sight of the Creator, the believer and the disbeliever, the righteous and the wicked, are equal—meaning their life and death are identical. It states: if this is their concept, this is a highly bad decision they are making. This is contrary to that sense of justice which is imbued within human nature. No matter how bad a life a person leads, he respects goodness. The one who looks at affairs impartially never agrees that the righteous and the wicked are dealt with identically. In the laws made by humans in every era, this principle of justice has been kept; so how can it be assumed regarding Allah Almighty that He is devoid of it? If the righteous and the wicked are equal in His sight, it means He is—God forbid—unjust and unfair, or He is indifferent to goodness and evil, and these are things which cannot be conceived regarding Him. And if He is Just, it is necessary that He brings a Day in which He judges between people—rewarding those who led a life of goodness and justice, and punishing those who led a life of rebellion. The coming of such a Day is also necessary because this world is operating on the law of trial. Both the righteous and the wicked have been granted respite to choose whatever life they please; therefore, He does not have to punish the wicked or reward the righteous here. He has appointed a specific Day for this. The Qur'an has come to warn of that punishment and give glad tidings of that reward; those who mock it assume that the Creator is a mere player who is observing the play of this world, having no concern with its good and evil.

"And Allah created the heavens and the earth in truth and so that every soul may be recompensed for what it earned, and they will not be wronged." (22)

Contrary to Their Assumption, the Creator Created This World in Truth

That is, they have assumed so, but Allah Almighty created the heavens and the earth with a purpose and truth. The requirement of this purpose is that a Day comes in which Allah Almighty judges between people—recompensing every soul for what it did, without any injustice. If no such Day comes, and this world continues as it is or ends in vain one day—neither the righteous receiving any reward nor the wicked any punishment—it means this world is reduced to a plaything of a player, and He created this vast workshop without any purpose.

After 'in truth' (bil-haqq), some words are omitted due to contextual clarity—meaning: 'that He may judge between them'. 'and so that every soul may be recompensed' (walitujza kullu nafsin) is conjoined with this omitted clause.

'and they will not be wronged' is meant to point to the reality: on that Day, everyone will face complete justice, without any injustice. If any room for injustice is left, it means this world was created in vain. This was said to negate those false concepts of shirk and intercession in which the polytheists and the People of the Book were caught. Believing in the Hereafter alongside these concepts was equivalent to denying it. When the belief is that regardless of deeds, partners and intercessors will save them, where remains the complete justice! Then, the way for every injustice is opened.

"Have you seen he who has taken as his god his [own] desire, and Allah sent him astray on the basis of knowledge and has set a seal upon his hearing and his heart and put over his vision a veil? So who will guide him after Allah? Then will you not be reminded?" (23)

A Timely Reference to the Jews

In the preceding verse, the polytheists' wrong concept of life was reproached. In this verse, regarding the Jews who supported the polytheists, the Muslims are assured: 'The cause of their supporting the polytheists despite claiming to hold the Book is that as a retribution for their bad deeds, Allah has sealed over their ears and hearts and placed a veil over their eyes. So, those who have deserved this under the law of God, who can guide them?'

'Have you seen he who has taken as his god his [own] desire' (Afara'ayta mani-ttakhadha ilahahu hawah)—'afara'ayta' comes to draw attention with wonder or regret, just as we say: 'have you seen so and so?' Attention is drawn to the Jews' state with wonder because Allah Almighty gave them His Book and Shariah, but they threw them behind their backs to make their own desires and innovations their deities. When a person becomes such a slave of desires that he does not care about His explicit commands, but finds His good in his desires alone, it means he is the servant of his desires, not of God. This has passed in Surah Al-Baqarah.

'and Allah sent him astray on the basis of knowledge'—meaning their deprivation is not because they did not receive the light of knowledge; rather, their misfortune is that despite receiving the knowledge, they chose to follow their desires and innovations. As a retribution, Allah left them to wander in deviation. Above, He stated: 'And We certainly gave the Children of Israel the Scripture... And they did not become divided until after knowledge had come to them - out of jealous animosity between themselves.' What was stated there with 'until after knowledge had come to them' is stated here with 'on the basis of knowledge' ('ala 'ilmin). Meaning: these people have been left to wander in the darkness after receiving the light, so do not expect any good of them.

'and has set a seal upon his hearing and his heart and put over his vision a veil'—exactly this has passed in Surah Al-Baqarah under the very same verse, and we have commented on it. The Qur'an has described specifically the Jews with this attribute.

'So who will guide him after Allah? Then will you not be reminded?'—before 'after Allah' (min ba'di-llah), a prefixed noun is omitted—meaning: 'after Allah has left him astray'. The meaning is: who can guide the one whose heart has been sealed under the Sunnah of Allah! The law of Allah Almighty regarding guidance and deviation has been explained. Have a look at the discussion on the sealing of hearts in Surah Al-Baqarah.

'Then will you not be reminded?'—this is advice and warning to the Muslims: 'Why do you wonder that these educated people who claim to hold the Book have stood up as the enemies of the Qur'an and the companions of the polytheists? Remember that Allah Almighty guides only those who value His guidance. Those who do not, the guidance becomes a trap of deviation for them.' This has been explained under 'He misleads many thereby and guides many thereby'.

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 24–37

Next, He commented on the deniers' demand: when they are warned of the Resurrection, they demand to see their deceased ancestors raised alive. Refuting this, He depicted that end which the deniers of the Resurrection must face, and clarified: on that Day, no partner will save from His grasp, and success will be the share only of those who walk upon the path of faith and righteous deeds, instead of relying on false intercession. Recite the verses.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"And they say, 'There is not but our worldly life; we die and we live, and nothing destroys us except time.' And they have of that no knowledge; they are only assuming." (24)

The Deniers' Claim

In verses 21-22, the moral, natural, and cosmic proofs of the Resurrection have passed. Now, this is a reference to the claim which the deniers presented in opposition. It states: when they are reminded of the Resurrection, they claim: 'Life is limited only to this worldly life. After this, there is neither death nor life. Those who warn us of life after death and reckoning are caught in an illusion.'

'we die and we live'—meaning our dying and living is limited only to this world. After dying, the entire story ends.

'and nothing destroys us except time'—and it is also entirely false that God causes us to die and will gather us to reckon and reward or punish us. God has no concern with this! It is only the alternation of times that destroys us. Just as a tree grows, matures, and dries to perish, we are born, and then by some turn of times, die in childhood, youth, or old age.

'And they have of that no knowledge; they are only assuming'—this is Allah Almighty's commentary: what they say is not based on any knowledge, but they are throwing random guesses. They do not want to believe in the Resurrection, as it restricts their freedom and ruins their enjoyment; because of this, they say these things without any thought, denying a reality whose testimony is present within human nature, in the system of this universe, and in His attributes of justice, wisdom, mercy, and power. If it does not occur, this world is reduced to a plaything of a player.

Some have derived from this verse the conclusion that there was a group of atheists in Arabia who denied God and the Resurrection, but in our view, this is incorrect. Individuals are a different matter, but as a group, no such group existed at that time who denied God and the Resurrection. The Arabs did not deny, but committed shirk, and through this shirk, made God a marginalized entity in the system of the world. In the matter of the Resurrection, they were in doubt. Their thought was: being raised alive after death is highly improbable, and if we are raised, our matter is with our deities, who will secure high ranks for us, or save us from Him.

Their statement: 'nothing destroys us except time' (wa ma yuhlikuna illa-d-dahr) was not a negation of God, but a negation of that philosophy of history which the Qur'an narrated to the Quraysh—showing that previous nations were destroyed as a retribution for their bad deeds and denial of the messengers. 'If you adopt their way, you will face their end.' This was based on the truth; because of this, those who possessed foresight were influenced. They feared that the Qur'an's word was true, and if they denied this invitation, they might face some such punishment as the Qur'an warned of. To keep people satisfied, the leaders of the Quraysh fabricated this philosophy: 'it is entirely false that previous nations were destroyed as a retribution for their bad deeds and denial. God has no concern with this; we perish due to the alternation of times. A person is born, matures, and dies, and this has no relation with his deeds, but is due to the alternation of times. Similarly, nations come into existence, progress, become the founders of civilizations, conquer, and one day perish. This play is occurring and will occur on the stage of the world; these are the miracles of the alternation of times. Those who connect this with deeds are superstitious and are putting people in doubts.' Have a look at what we wrote under 'There touched them adversity and hardship' (Al-A'raf: 95).

Do not assume that this jahl-philosophy has perished. In this era as well, this very philosophy is dominant over minds, and dominant over the minds of those who claim to believe in the Qur'an. If they are reminded: the afflictions that came or are coming are the results of our moral corruption, and if this continues, our ship will sink, their pride is hurt, and they reply: 'Such turns of times come upon nations; they came upon our predecessors, so why should it be assumed that this is the result of our corruption!'

'And they have of that no knowledge; they are only assuming'—meaning they say this with great pride, but they have no knowledge in this, only assumption on which they have built this philosophy. Reason, nature, the horizons, and the teachings of the prophets and wise are what the Qur'an presents, but these people choose to follow assumption instead of knowledge, because their desires can be validated only by assumption, not by knowledge. This expression is of regret—showing: unfortunate indeed are those who made assumption their guide in such a great matter.

"And when Our clear verses are recited to them, their argument is only that they say, 'Bring [back] our forefathers, if you should be truthful.'" (25)

The Sole Shield of the Disbelievers against the Proofs of the Qur'an

This explains that assumption of theirs which they made their guide against the clear proofs of the Qur'an. It states: when Our highly clear verses are recited to them, the only thing they present in response is their demand to the Prophet (pbuh) and his companions: 'If you are truthful in your claim, bring back our deceased ancestors.'

'their argument' (hujjatahum) is the predicate of 'kana'. This same theme has passed in Surah Al-Ankabut in slightly different words: 'their argument was only that they said, "Bring us the punishment of Allah, if you should be truthful."' (29). The Qur'an has declared this to be their sole argument, and after referring to it, has ignored it. This points to the fact: their sole argument, of which they are highly proud, is such that its absurdity is entirely obvious, having no relation to the actual discussion, nor to reason; but they are in the illusion that they have escaped what the Qur'an is warning of.

"Say, 'Allah causes you to live, then causes you to die; then He will gather you for the Day of Resurrection, about which there is no doubt, but most of the people do not know.'" (26)

The Qur'an's Reply

This is the reply of the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: you are not being told that whoever you demand will be raised alive before you today; but what is being stated is: Allah gives you life, then causes you to die, then He will gather you, and this gathering will continue until the Day of Judgment, whose coming is certain.

Meaning, when life and death are in the control of Allah alone, and this is an accepted reality, then the question of whether He can raise people alive after death is out of the discussion. Who created them can recreate them. And when He alone causes them to die, and no one else does, if He intends to recreate them, who has the strength to obstruct His will! Obstruction could occur only if life was in the control of one, and death of another; but proofs show that this universe is pure from such duality, testifying that a single will is operating upon it.

'then He will gather you for the Day of Resurrection' describes the requirement of life and death—stating: if after this, no Day of reward and punishment comes, this entire workshop is reduced to a plaything, so it is necessary that Allah Almighty gathers everyone, and this gathering will continue until the Day of Judgment, whose coming is necessary for life and death to be purposeful, and in whose coming there is no doubt.

After 'He will gather you' (yajma'ukum), the preposition 'ila' is to show the connection and continuity—meaning: this gathering will continue until the Resurrection, which also showed that no deceased will be raised before the Resurrection, nor has any such claim been made by the Qur'an or the Prophet; therefore, their demand to raise their ancestors alive is meaningless.

'but most of the people do not know'—this is an expression of wonder over the deniers: what they are being warned of is certain, but most of them do not know what a terrifying end they are preparing for themselves by denying this obvious reality.

"And to Allah belongs the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth. And the Day the Hour is established - that Day the falsifiers will lose." (27)

That is: if these people rely on their assumed partners to save them from His grasp, this is a mere assumption. The sovereignty of the heavens and the earth is in the control of Allah alone; no one has the power to affect His decisions. When that Day comes, those who remained in these false hopes will face ruin.

"And you will see every nation kneeling [from fear]. Every nation will be called to its record, [and it will be said], 'This Day you will be recompensed for what you used to do.'" (28)

Depicting the Day of Judgment

The state of the people on that Day is depicted—stating: on that Day, every group will sit kneeling on its knees to hear its decision, and every group will be called to its record of deeds, and they will be told: 'Today you will receive in return only what you did in the world.'

'You will see' (Tara), though singular, is general, and the context is proof. 

'every nation' (kulla ummatin)—meaning believers, disbelievers, righteous, and wicked, all will be gathered on that Day; no one will be exempted from this presentation.

'Kneeling' (Jathiyah) is from 'jatha ya'joo'. 'Jatha al-rajul' has been explained by lexicographers as: 'He sat on his knees.' Slaves, subjects, and criminals sat in this manner before their masters and rulers to hear their decisions.

'Record' (Al-Kitab) refers here to the record of deeds. This is because this register contains the record of all deeds of the groups; therefore, it is suitable to describe it as a record. Its explanation will follow in Surah Al-Mutaffifeen, where the names of these registers are also mentioned.

Before 'This Day you will be recompensed' (Al-yawma tujzawna), 'it will be said to them' (qeela lahum) is omitted due to contextual clarity—meaning every group will be called to its record of deeds and told: 'What you sowed in the world, today you will receive in return.'

The meaning is: the one who did nothing, but relied entirely on shirk and intercession to dream sweet dreams, will have nothing but deprivation on that Day.

"This, Our record, speaks about you in truth. Indeed, We were having transcribed whatever you used to do." (29)

This is also a part of the preceding statement—meaning they will be informed: no injustice or oversight is to be feared from this record. It will state exactly what everyone did, because everyone's record is present in writing. We continuously had the angels record whatever you did.

"So as for those who believed and did righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy. That is the clear attainment." (30)

After informing every group of its record of deeds, this describes their individual end. First, the end of the believers is described—stating: those who led a life of faith and righteous deeds will be admitted by their Lord into His mercy. 'Mercy' points to the reality: they will not only receive the recompense of their deeds, but also His endless favor. After this, by way of praise, He stated: 'The clear success is this which these people will achieve, and not that temporary comfort of the world behind which those foolish ones lost this eternal kingdom!'

"But as for those who disbelieved, [it will be said], 'Were not Our verses recited to you, but you were arrogant and became a people [who were] criminals?'" (31)

This describes the end of the disbelievers, and their end is described in detail compared to the believers. The primary discussion in this Surah is with the disbelievers, and specifically with the arrogant among them—stating: as for those who disbelieved, they will be asked: 'Is it not a fact: when Our verses were recited to you to warn you of this Day, you showed arrogance and persisted on your crimes!' The details of this arrogance and persistence have passed in verses 8-9 and 24-25. Asking them this question is not to find an answer, but to reproach and disgrace them.

"And when it was said, 'Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth and the Hour [is coming], no doubt in it,' you said, 'We do not know what is the Hour. We assume not except assumption, and we are not convinced.'" (32)

The Deniers' Foolish Demand

This explains their arrogance—showing: 'When you were told that God's promise of reward and punishment is true and there is no doubt in the Resurrection, you replied with arrogance: "We do not know what is the Hour; we only assume, and we are not convinced."'

This shows that as far as assumption is concerned, these deniers also possessed it in their hearts, but their demand was: they would believe only when they achieved full certainty, and for this certainty, their demand was: 'Bring back our forefathers, if you should be truthful.' Obviously, this was a highly foolish demand. It meant: a person should believe only what he has seen with his eyes, and should not believe anything else, even if highly clear rational and moral proofs are present in its favor. If a person falls to this, then intellect becomes a redundant thing; rather, no difference remains between him and a beast except of form. This would mean he does not even accept the existence of intellect, because he has neither seen nor touched intellect. If you evaluate the sciences of man, you will find that a major part of them is based on rational and moral principles. If the physicalist perspective of these arrogant ones is accepted, it means all those sciences which man has produced are to be wiped out.

The Way of Wisdom

If you reflect, in such important matters of far-reaching consequences, the guidance of dominant assumption (zann-e-ghalib) is sufficient. A massive dam, whose breaking could threaten the entire city, does not demand our attention when it breaks; but wise people remain alert to such matters beforehand. The matter of the Hereafter is a highly important matter, rather the most important matter of this entire universe. The proofs of it described in the Qur'an and other scriptures are undeniable. At most, what anyone can say is: he does not possess that certainty which he has regarding what he has seen with his eyes. If he does not possess such certainty, let him not; let him see whether he can claim with full certainty that there is no Hereafter, and obviously, no one can claim this. In such conditions, wisdom requires that a person compares which of the two paths is of safety. That a person leaves the thought of the Hereafter to follow the desires of his self and does not reflect on what will happen after death, or that he believes in the Hereafter to lead a life keeping reward and punishment before him, even if he has to sacrifice some of his desires for this. If you reflect, wisdom requires that he chooses this second path, because in choosing the first path lies an eternal danger, and the benefit is no more than being free to live according to your desires, though their fulfillment is not in your control but in the control of another.

On the contrary, in this second path there is no danger. If the Hereafter occurs, you will achieve an eternal kingdom, and if it does not, what is the loss? At most, you had to sacrifice some temporary desires in this temporary life, and their fulfillment was also not in your control, but in the control of another.

"And the evil consequences of what they did will appear to them, and they will be enveloped by what they used to mock." (33)

The Actual Results of the Wicked Will Appear in the Hereafter

Those intellectual and moral crimes which a person commits in this world, their bad results do not appear immediately; because of this, he becomes bold, and let alone accepting the reformers' advice, he considers them foolish and mocks their words. On the Day of Judgment, Allah Almighty will remove the veil from the actual results of deeds; on that Day, even the blindest will see what a poisonous crop he sowed in the world, and of whose end he was warned by the messengers and His good servants, but he did not care. On that Day, the punishment which he mocked with great audacity will envelop him in such a way that no way of escape will remain.

"And it will be said, 'This Day We will forget you as you forgot the meeting of this Day of yours, and your refuge is the Fire, and for you there are no helpers.'" (34)

'Nasiya' (forget) is used here in the sense of ignoring. That is, they will be informed at the very first stage: 'Just as in the world you ignored the messengers and the good servants of Allah, and when they warned you of this Day of meeting, you turned away; similarly, today We will ignore you. No matter how much you cry, you will not be heard with Us; and remember also: if you were proud of your numbers, they cannot help you today, and if you relied on your partners, they will be of no use.'

"That is because you took the verses of Allah in ridicule, and the worldly life deceived you. So this Day they will not be brought out of it, nor will they be asked to appease [Allah]." (35)

That is, you deserved this because when the verses of Allah were recited to you to warn you of this Day, you mocked them, and departed as if you heard nothing. Verses 8-9 have passed.

'and the worldly life deceived you'—meaning the comfort and tharwat which Allah Almighty bestowed upon you in the world led you to the conclusion: you are deserving of it, and your tharwat is proof that there is no deficiency in your beliefs or deeds. In this pride, you mocked those who tried to draw your attention to your moral corruption, asking: 'Are our conditions better or yours? When our conditions are better than yours, why should we not assume that our beliefs and deeds are better and the fault is within your minds.'

'So this Day they will not be brought out of it, nor will they be asked to appease [Allah]'—it states: in retribution for this pride of theirs, neither will they be allowed to go out of Hell, nor will they be given the opportunity to seek forgiveness of their Lord to please Him. Rather, all doors of hope will be closed. In this clause, the sudden change of style is noteworthy: the preceding clause was in the second-person style, and this clause is in the third-person style; as if the warning of ignoring them has begun, to the extent that they no longer deserve address. It should be kept in mind that the third-person style is used in places of ignoring. Precedents of this have passed.

"Then to Allah belongs [all] praise, Lord of the heavens and Lord of the earth, Lord of the worlds. And to Him belongs [all] majesty within the heavens and the earth, and He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise." (36-37)

Summary of the Discussion

At the end, this places the summary of the entire discussion before us: when these realities are entirely clear, then the deserving recipient of gratitude and praise is that Allah Who is the Lord of the heavens, the Lord of the earth, and the Lord of the worlds. The meaning is: then, leaving Him, from where did you derive other lords; how did you appoint different deities of the heavens and the earth? and being His creation and His servants, why did you fabricate so many deities?

'And to Him belongs [all] majesty...'—this is the second requirement: when the Sovereign of this entire universe is He alone, then from where did the majesty of any other in the heavens or the earth arise? Then, He alone is the deserving recipient of all majesty, and everyone must bow down before Him. If anyone raises their head as His rival, they challenge His majesty, and whoever challenges His majesty will necessarily face retribution.

'the Exalted in Might'—He is dominant and powerful; therefore, no one can escape His grasp. But along with this, He is Wise; therefore, if He has granted people respite, let no one remain in the misconception that they have escaped His grasp; His every action is based on wisdom, and this wisdom will stand manifest one day.

With the favor and guidance of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed on these lines. Praise be to Allah for His favor.
